behaviors.description: The generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type) specified in the description.
behaviors.justification: Signature correctness depends entirely on what the real LLM produces. A mock LLM configured to return valid code would always pass, hiding real failures where the actual LLM generates functions with mismatched parameter names, incorrect types, or wrong return type. This is non-deterministic—the LLM may produce different signatures on different runs. Since the failure boundary is LLM output variability, not infrastructure or loop logic, this requires a real LLM call (Agentic level).
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM API with a detailed prompt specifying parameter names, types, and return type. Parse the generated code and verify that the function signature exactly matches the specification. Run this test multiple times to capture LLM non-determinism and ensure signatures consistently match across runs.

behaviors.description: The generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt.
behaviors.justification: This behavior explicitly requires multiple steps in sequence: generate code, execute tests, and if tests fail, refine via a second (or third) LLM call. Testing a single LLM call would miss failures in the refinement loop—the real failure is that refinement doesn't improve code quality or the loop terminates incorrectly. Also requires real test execution (subprocess-level) to determine if generated code actually works. The phrase 'first or a subsequent attempt' indicates multi-step iteration is core, not incidental.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM to generate a function. Execute it against provided test cases via subprocess or sandbox. If tests fail, pass the test failure messages back to the LLM and iterate (up to 3 times). Verify that code passes tests on the first attempt or after refinement. Run with functions known to be difficult for single-pass generation.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes the failure messages in the next LLM prompt so the model can learn from its mistakes.
behaviors.justification: The core behavior being tested is deterministic: does the refinement prompt include the test failure messages? This is a prompt-construction property testable by mocking both the LLM and test executor, then inspecting the second LLM call's arguments. No mock-miss here—we can verify the error messages are formatted correctly and inserted into the prompt without needing the real LLM. The real LLM's ability to use that context is covered by behavior 5 (end-to-end refinement success).
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM and test executor. Call the code generation service with a prompt. Trigger a failure in the first LLM's generated code (via mocked test failure). Capture the second LLM prompt argument and verify it includes the test failure messages, exception types, and error context in a readable format.

behaviors.description: The service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error with all three attempts and their test results.
behaviors.justification: This tests loop control logic and response structure, both deterministic. Mock the LLM to return failing code and mock test results to return failures—then verify that the loop terminates exactly after 3 iterations and the error response includes all three attempts with test results. This doesn't depend on real LLM output quality or real test execution variability; it depends on the service's bookkeeping logic, which is testable with mocks.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM and test executor such that they return failures predictably. Invoke the service and verify: (1) loop stops after exactly 3 iterations, (2) returned error is a structured response containing all three attempts, (3) each attempt includes the generated code and test failure details. Also test the edge case where attempt 3 succeeds—verify the service returns success, not an error.

behaviors.description: The full loop — generate, test, refine, test again — produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong.
behaviors.justification: This is the end-to-end correctness test for the core value proposition. A mock LLM configured to generate passing code would hide real failures in refinement—the real failure is that the LLM generates incorrect code but the refinement loop fails to fix it. Requires real LLM calls because refinement ability is non-deterministic and context-dependent. Requires multiple agentic steps in sequence (generate, test, refine, test, refine again). Cannot be tested with mocks without defeating the purpose of verifying refinement works.
behaviors.test_description: Choose function descriptions known to be difficult for single-pass generation (e.g., complex algorithms, edge cases). Call the real LLM to generate initial code. Execute against tests via subprocess. If tests fail, pass failures to the LLM and iterate (up to 3 times, with real LLM calls). Verify that either the first attempt passes or a subsequent refinement produces passing code. Run multiple times to account for LLM non-determinism.
